chapters.title: Anatomy, Physiology, Development, and Scientific Foundations
chapters.summary: Connect speech, language, hearing, cognition, feeding, and swallowing performance with relevant anatomy, physiology, development, neurology, and epidemiology.
objectives: Explain the coordinated speech and swallowing subsystems.
Relate nervous-system structures to communication function.
Use developmental and epidemiologic concepts without overgeneralizing.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Respiration, phonation, and resonance
sections.content: Speech begins with coordinated respiratory support. Expiratory airflow and subglottal pressure interact with vocal-fold position and tissue properties to create phonation, while the vocal tract shapes the acoustic signal. The velopharyngeal valve helps direct sound energy and oral pressure; dysfunction can affect resonance and pressure consonants. Describe subsystem interactions rather than assigning an auditory symptom to one structure without examination.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Oral, pharyngeal, and cranial-nerve function
sections.content: Cranial nerves support facial sensation and mastication, facial expression and lip seal, hearing and balance, pharyngeal sensation and movement, laryngeal and palatal function, and tongue movement. Oral preparation, bolus transport, airway protection, and speech share structures but use different timing and coordination. A cranial-nerve finding is one piece of a neurologic and functional assessment, not a stand-alone medical diagnosis.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Neural systems and communication
sections.content: Language, motor speech, cognition, hearing, and swallowing depend on distributed networks rather than isolated centers. Acquired injury can disrupt linguistic formulation, speech planning, neuromuscular execution, attention, memory, or executive control in different combinations. Experience-dependent neuroplasticity supports rehabilitation, but recovery varies with lesion, health, timing, practice, environment, motivation, and the functional relevance of the task.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Development, variation, and epidemiology
sections.content: Typical development is a range shaped by maturation, interaction, language exposure, culture, sensory access, health, and opportunity. Prelinguistic joint attention, gesture, turn taking, and babble support later language, while speech, literacy, and discourse continue developing over years. Incidence counts new cases during a period; prevalence describes existing cases. Neither a milestone nor a population estimate should replace individualized assessment.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which cranial nerve is most directly responsible for tongue movement during speech and swallowing?
choices: The hypoglossal nerve, cranial nerve XII.
The optic nerve, cranial nerve II.
The vestibulocochlear nerve, cranial nerve VIII.
The accessory nerve, cranial nerve XI.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Cranial nerve XII supplies the intrinsic and most extrinsic tongue muscles, so weakness can affect articulation and bolus control.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which cranial nerve provides major motor innervation to the muscles of facial expression?
choices: The trigeminal nerve, cranial nerve V.
The facial nerve, cranial nerve VII.
The glossopharyngeal nerve, cranial nerve IX.
The vagus nerve, cranial nerve X.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Cranial nerve VII supplies the muscles of facial expression and contributes to lip closure and facial symmetry.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What aerodynamic condition is generally required to initiate and sustain voiced phonation?
choices: Complete absence of airflow through the glottis.
Continuous elevation of the soft palate without vocal-fold adduction.
Adequate respiratory support and subglottal pressure sufficient to set the vocal folds into vibration.
Maximal tongue-base retraction throughout the utterance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Voice depends on coordinated respiration, phonation, and resonance; airflow and pressure interact with vocal-fold position and tissue properties.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the primary speech consequence of velopharyngeal insufficiency?
choices: A purely receptive-language impairment.
Bilateral sensorineural hearing loss.
Isolated word-finding difficulty.
Excessive nasal resonance and reduced oral pressure for pressure consonants.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: The velopharyngeal valve directs acoustic energy and oral air pressure; incomplete closure can cause hypernasality and nasal air emission.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which observation is most consistent with typical early communication development?
choices: Joint attention, turn taking, gesture, and increasingly varied babble develop before and alongside first words.
Reading comprehension consistently precedes joint attention.
Complex syntax normally appears before intentional gesture.
Phonological awareness is fully mature during canonical babbling.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Prelinguistic social interaction, gesture, vocal play, and babbling provide foundations for later speech and language.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Professional Practice, Ethics, Law, and Interprofessional Care
chapters.summary: Apply scope, competence, consent, confidentiality, documentation, supervision, school law, telepractice, and team-based decision principles.
objectives: Recognize scope and competence boundaries.
Distinguish consent, privacy, and documentation duties by setting.
Coordinate ethical school and health-care services.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Scope, competence, and ethical reasoning
sections.content: Practice within the current SLP scope and within personal competence based on education, training, supervised experience, and available resources. When a task exceeds competence, obtain qualified supervision, collaborate, or refer. For an ethical dilemma, define the stakeholders and power, identify current ethics and law, examine alternatives and foreseeable consequences, consult appropriately, act, document, and evaluate rather than relying on habit or hierarchy.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Consent, privacy, and documentation
sections.content: Informed consent explains nature, purpose, expected benefit, material risk, alternatives, limits, and voluntary choice in accessible language. Privacy rules depend on setting: FERPA often governs school education records, while HIPAA applies in covered health-care contexts, with state law and organizational policy adding requirements. Document relevant status, services, response, data, interpretation, communication, and plans accurately, promptly, securely, and without cloning unsupported details.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: School eligibility and educational impact
sections.content: A clinical diagnosis does not automatically create IDEA eligibility, and school eligibility is not a medical diagnosis. The multidisciplinary team applies current criteria and examines whether a disability affects educational performance broadly and requires special education or related services. Use multiple nondiscriminatory tools, the student’s native language or communication mode when feasible, functional classroom evidence, family input, and all suspected areas without relying on one score.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Collaboration, supervision, and telepractice
sections.content: Interprofessional care clarifies roles, communicates scope-relevant findings, coordinates compatible goals, and centers the individual’s priorities. A qualified supervisor matches delegated tasks to competence and retains accountability under current rules. Telepractice requires authority where the client is located, privacy and accessibility safeguards, informed consent, local emergency procedures, technology contingencies, and confirmation that remote service is clinically appropriate.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Before initiating a nonemergency evaluation or treatment, what should informed consent include?
choices: The nature, purpose, expected benefits, material risks, alternatives, limits, and the person’s opportunity to ask questions and decide voluntarily.
A signature obtained before any explanation.
A guarantee that the recommended treatment will succeed.
Only the clinician’s preferred option.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Consent is an ongoing communication process, not merely a form, and requirements vary with setting, law, capacity, and service.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: An SLP is asked to use an unfamiliar instrumental procedure without adequate training. What is the best response?
choices: Proceed because equipment software produces a score.
Seek qualified training and supervision, collaborate or refer as needed, and do not practice beyond competence.
Ask an assistant to perform it without oversight.
Use the procedure and omit its limitations from the report.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Ethical practice requires competence based on education, training, experience, and appropriate consultation or referral.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which privacy framework should an SLP apply when sharing records?
choices: Assume HIPAA applies identically in every school.
Assume FERPA permits access to every employee.
Determine which law and policy govern the setting, verify authority and purpose, obtain consent when required, and disclose only authorized information securely.
Share the complete record whenever another professional asks.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: FERPA, HIPAA, state law, consent, and organizational policy can apply differently; employment alone does not create unlimited access.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What makes clinical documentation defensible?
choices: A copied note that does not reflect the session.
A vague statement that the client did well.
Only a billing code without clinical information.
Objective, timely documentation of relevant status, procedures, response, data, interpretation, plan, and communication.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Documentation should accurately represent services and decisions while protecting privacy and meeting applicable legal and organizational requirements.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: An SLP has a financial relationship with a company whose device is being recommended. What should the SLP do?
choices: Disclose the relevant conflict, preserve objective feature matching and alternatives, and follow ethics and organizational policy.
Hide the relationship because the device may help.
Recommend the highest-priced option automatically.
Let the vendor determine candidacy.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Transparency and independent clinical judgment protect the individual from exploitation and support informed choice.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Evidence-Based and Culturally Responsive Practice
chapters.summary: Integrate research, clinical expertise, person priorities, measurement quality, linguistic evidence, and equitable access into decisions.
objectives: Form answerable clinical questions and appraise evidence.
Interpret reliability, validity, effect, and uncertainty.
Differentiate language difference from disorder.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: The evidence-based practice triad
sections.content: Evidence-based practice integrates best available external research, clinical expertise and data, and the individual’s values, communication preferences, culture, circumstances, goals, and risk tolerance. A PICO question specifies population or problem, intervention, comparison when relevant, and meaningful outcome. Shared decision making explains benefits, harms, burden, uncertainty, and alternatives so evidence is applied with the person rather than imposed on the person.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Research design and causal claims
sections.content: Match design to the claim. Randomized trials can strengthen causal inference; well-designed single-case experiments demonstrate repeated functional relations; cohort and case-control studies address prognosis and association; qualitative work can examine experience and implementation. Appraise selection, attrition, fidelity, outcome validity, confounding, effect size, precision, adverse events, replication, and applicability before translating a group result to an individual.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Reliability, validity, and clinical importance
sections.content: Reliability concerns consistency and precision under relevant conditions. Validity is evidence supporting a particular score interpretation and use; a reliable measure can still be invalid for the decision. Confidence intervals convey uncertainty around an estimate. Statistical significance does not establish magnitude or practical benefit, so examine effect size, precision, meaningful change, burden, cost, equity, and whether the measured outcome matters to the person.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Culturally and linguistically responsive decisions
sections.content: Collect language, dialect, education, migration, hearing, opportunity, and community information without treating difference as deficit. Assess all relevant languages with qualified interpreters, representative samples, observation, dynamic methods, and measures whose limits are documented. Test–teach–retest can reveal modifiability. Do not retain standard scores after unvalidated translation, and require converging difficulty not explained by exposure or dialect before diagnosing disorder.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which elements make up evidence-based practice in speech-language pathology?
choices: Best available research, clinical expertise, and the individual’s values, preferences, culture, circumstances, and goals.
Published research alone regardless of fit.
Clinician habit alone.
A family preference without information about benefits or risks.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Evidence-based practice integrates multiple sources through shared decision making rather than treating any single source as sufficient.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the purpose of a PICO question?
choices: List only a diagnosis and preferred test.
Specify the population or problem, intervention, comparison when relevant, and outcome.
Replace outcome measurement with expert opinion.
Guarantee that one study will answer every case question.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: PICO helps focus searching and appraisal by connecting a defined population, intervention, comparison, and meaningful outcome.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which study design best supports a causal claim about an intervention when feasible and ethical?
choices: An uncontrolled testimonial.
A cross-sectional prevalence survey.
A well-conducted randomized controlled trial with valid outcomes, fidelity monitoring, and appropriate analysis.
A case history without repeated measurement.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Random assignment can strengthen causal inference, but attrition, implementation, measurement, applicability, and ethics still require appraisal.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A measure produces highly consistent scores but does not assess the intended construct. How should it be described?
choices: Reliability automatically proves validity.
The measure is valid because it is standardized.
Consistency makes construct definition unnecessary.
The measure may be reliable but lacks validity evidence for the intended interpretation and use.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Reliability concerns precision; validity concerns evidence supporting a particular interpretation and decision in context.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why should effect size and confidence intervals accompany a statistical-significance result?
choices: Magnitude, precision, practical importance, study quality, adverse effects, and relevance to the individual.
Assume statistical significance guarantees meaningful benefit.
Ignore uncertainty because the p value is below a threshold.
Treat the average effect as identical for every participant.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Statistical significance alone does not establish clinical importance, precision, causality, applicability, or individual response.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Disorder Characteristics, Etiology, and Differential Reasoning
chapters.summary: Recognize defining patterns across the big-nine practice areas while separating description, etiology, diagnosis, and functional impact.
objectives: Compare major speech and motor-speech patterns.
Describe language, cognition, social communication, AAC, and hearing needs.
Recognize feeding and swallowing risk without overdiagnosis.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Speech sound and fluency patterns
sections.content: A class-based sound substitution may reflect a phonological pattern, while difficulty with a particular motor placement may be articulatory; both require developmental, linguistic, hearing, structural, motor, stimulability, and intelligibility evidence. Fluency assessment distinguishes typical disfluency, stuttering-like repetitions, prolongations and blocks, and cluttering features while examining tension, anticipation, avoidance, communication impact, variability, and speaker identity and goals.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Voice, resonance, and motor speech
sections.content: Persistent dysphonia may arise from structural, neurologic, inflammatory, behavioral, or other causes and requires appropriate laryngeal evaluation. Hypernasality and nasal emission can reflect velopharyngeal dysfunction, sometimes with learned compensatory articulations. Dysarthria affects neuromuscular execution across speech subsystems; apraxia of speech affects planning and programming and may show inconsistent distortions, groping, segmentation, and prosodic disruption.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Language, cognition, social communication, AAC, and hearing
sections.content: Developmental language disorder can affect vocabulary, grammar, narrative, discourse, and literacy. Aphasia is acquired multimodal language impairment. Cognitive-communication disorders link attention, memory, executive function, self-monitoring, discourse, and participation. Social communication is evaluated in cultural and contextual terms. AAC is a communication modality, not a diagnosis, and hearing access interacts with language, technology, environment, and communication mode.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Feeding, swallowing, and etiology
sections.content: Dysphagia may involve oral preparation, oral transport, pharyngeal, or esophageal contributors and can affect airway protection, nutrition, hydration, efficiency, and participation. Cough, wet voice, prolonged meals, recurrent respiratory concerns, or poor growth may signal need for evaluation, but no single sign defines physiology and aspiration may be silent. Etiologies can be developmental, neurologic, structural, genetic, disease related, auditory, functional, or mixed.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: A child consistently substitutes stops for fricatives across many words. What does this pattern most directly represent?
choices: A phonological pattern of stopping that should be interpreted with age, language, dialect, stimulability, and intelligibility data.
A receptive-language disorder by itself.
A vocal-fold lesion.
A pharyngeal swallowing impairment.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A systematic class-based sound change is phonological; diagnosis requires broader developmental, linguistic, motor, hearing, and functional evidence.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which feature most distinguishes stuttering from typical disfluency?
choices: Word-finding pauses alone establish stuttering.
Stuttering-like disfluencies and associated tension or impact warrant assessment of frequency, context, experience, participation, and variability.
Only total syllable count matters.
Stuttering can be diagnosed from listener discomfort alone.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Fluency assessment includes overt behaviors, secondary behaviors, anticipation, avoidance, emotional impact, communication participation, and the speaker’s goals.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which contrast best differentiates dysarthria from apraxia of speech?
choices: Both are purely language-comprehension disorders.
Apraxia is always caused by muscle weakness.
Dysarthria reflects impaired neuromuscular execution, whereas apraxia of speech primarily affects motor planning and programming.
Dysarthria affects writing but never speech.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Motor-speech differential diagnosis integrates strength, tone, coordination, consistency, prosody, articulation, phonation, respiration, and neurologic findings.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: After a left-hemisphere stroke, a client has impaired word retrieval, sentence formulation, and comprehension. What broad disorder is most likely?
choices: Developmental phonological disorder.
Isolated conductive hearing loss.
Velopharyngeal insufficiency.
Aphasia.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Aphasia is an acquired language disorder that can affect speaking, listening, reading, and writing in varying patterns and severities.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which feature is especially important in cognitive-communication disorders after traumatic brain injury?
choices: Communication breakdown can arise from impaired attention, memory, executive function, self-monitoring, and social cognition in functional contexts.
Normal articulation rules out communication disability.
Only confrontation naming should be assessed.
Cognition and communication should be treated as unrelated.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Cognitive processes support discourse, learning, organization, inference, self-regulation, and participation even when basic speech is intact.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Assessment Principles, Measurement, and Integrated Diagnosis
chapters.summary: Move from referral and screening through multimethod assessment, score interpretation, differential reasoning, reporting, and referral.
objectives: Distinguish screening from comprehensive evaluation.
Select and interpret measures for a defined purpose.
Integrate evidence into transparent recommendations.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Referral, history, screening, and triage
sections.content: Clarify the referral question and the decision that assessment must support. Gather relevant developmental, medical, sensory, educational, linguistic, cultural, service, participation, and person-priority history. Screening is brief risk identification, not diagnosis. Failed hearing screening, acute neurologic change, respiratory distress, inability to manage secretions, or other red flags require timely referral or emergency action under current protocols rather than routine completion of a test battery.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Selecting and interpreting measures
sections.content: Choose a tool because it measures the needed construct and has adequate reliability, validity, norm relevance, accessibility, sensitivity, specificity, and fairness for the intended decision. Standard error and confidence intervals discourage treating a score as exact. Criterion-referenced probes can identify skills and monitor mastery. Standardized administration supports comparison, but any accommodation or departure must be documented with its effect on interpretation.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Multimethod functional assessment
sections.content: Combine interview, observation, standardized and nonstandardized measures, language or speech samples, work products, oral-mechanism findings, dynamic assessment, and relevant instrumental procedures. Sample authentic partners, settings, purposes, fatigue, complexity, and supports. Conflicting sources can reveal contextual variation; compare task, informant, timing, access, and measure quality rather than averaging disagreement away or selecting the most severe score.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Integration, diagnosis, and reporting
sections.content: Answer each referral question with converging and conflicting evidence, functional strengths and needs, differential hypotheses, limitations, and uncertainty. Explain how findings affect participation, learning, health, or communication. Recommendations should be feasible, prioritized, culturally responsive, and connected to measurable outcomes, referrals, and follow-up. Use accessible language and shared decision making instead of score lists or unsupported certainty.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a screening?
choices: Screening identifies possible risk and need for follow-up; it does not by itself establish a diagnosis or eligibility.
The student has a confirmed disorder.
The result should be converted directly into a treatment plan.
No additional information is needed.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Screening is a brief risk-identification process whose follow-up depends on the tool, result, context, urgency, and governing procedures.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which case-history information is most useful before selecting assessment procedures?
choices: Only chronological age.
Developmental, medical, sensory, educational, linguistic, cultural, participation, family, prior-service, and client-priority information relevant to the referral.
Only the referring professional’s diagnostic label.
Only a single previous test score.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: A relevant case history helps define questions, identify risk and protective factors, select accessible procedures, and interpret findings.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should a failed hearing screening affect a speech-language evaluation?
choices: Diagnose language disorder without considering hearing.
Repeat language testing until the score increases.
Arrange timely audiologic or medical follow-up and interpret communication findings in light of uncertain auditory access.
Assume the hearing result is unrelated to communication.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Hearing status affects access to speech and language; follow-up and coordinated interpretation are essential.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the strongest basis for selecting a standardized test?
choices: The test is already on the shelf.
The score report is colorful.
The measure is popular regardless of population or purpose.
The measure addresses the referral question and has appropriate construct, reliability, validity, norm, accessibility, and fairness evidence for the intended use.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Test selection is purpose- and person-specific; standardization does not guarantee validity or fairness for every inference.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why should a confidence interval accompany a standard score?
choices: Measurement error and score precision; the obtained score is an estimate rather than an exact trait value.
The lower score is always clinically meaningful.
Confidence intervals prove a diagnosis.
Standard scores contain no measurement error.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Confidence intervals communicate uncertainty and help prevent overinterpretation of small or unstable differences.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Assessment of Speech Sound, Fluency, Voice, Resonance, and Motor Speech
chapters.summary: Use connected samples, subsystem tasks, medical coordination, and functional outcomes to differentiate speech, fluency, voice, resonance, and motor-speech needs.
objectives: Analyze speech sound patterns and intelligibility.
Assess fluency and voice beyond a single severity count.
Differentiate motor-speech and resonance mechanisms.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Speech sound production and intelligibility
sections.content: Collect single-word and connected-speech samples across word position, complexity, rate, language, dialect, and context. Analyze inventory, accuracy, phonological contrasts and patterns, consistency, stimulability, intelligibility, oral structure and movement, hearing, and language. A word test can miss breakdown under classroom, conversational, fatigue, or linguistic demands, so include listener and participation evidence and do not equate dialect with disorder.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Fluency experience and observable behavior
sections.content: Measure type, frequency, duration, and location of disfluencies across representative speaking situations, but also examine physical tension, secondary behaviors, rate, language organization, anticipation, avoidance, beliefs, emotions, identity, and participation. Clinic fluency may not represent school, work, telephone, or social demands. Center the speaker’s experience and goals rather than defining severity entirely through listener judgment.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Voice and resonance assessment
sections.content: Describe auditory-perceptual quality, pitch, loudness, effort, endurance, variability, and functional demand; collect acoustic or aerodynamic data when appropriate. Persistent dysphonia requires coordinated laryngeal medical evaluation because sound quality does not uniquely identify pathology. For resonance, examine oral structure, nasal emission, pressure consonants, articulation placement, and velopharyngeal function with team referral when structural information is needed.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Motor-speech differential diagnosis
sections.content: Sample oral motor status, sustained phonation, alternating and sequential motion rates, repeated utterances, increasing length, conversation, reading when appropriate, prosody, respiration, phonation, articulation, resonance, consistency, rate, fatigue, and intelligibility. Dysarthria patterns reflect execution across subsystems; apraxia patterns reflect planning and programming. Integrate neurologic, language, hearing, cognition, and task evidence before naming the pattern.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which evidence best distinguishes an articulation error from a phonological-pattern difficulty?
choices: Compare error consistency, sound classes, word positions, phonological contrasts, stimulability, intelligibility, and motor or structural findings.
Use age alone.
Count only correctly spelled words.
Administer a voice rating scale.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Speech-sound assessment integrates single-word and connected-speech data, pattern analysis, motor and structural status, hearing, language, and context.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why assess stimulability during a speech-sound evaluation?
choices: Whether the child has normal hearing.
Whether the sound can be elicited with models or cues, informing prognosis, target selection, and treatment planning.
Whether the child understands all vocabulary.
Whether the sound is mastered in spontaneous conversation.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Stimulability is one part of a broader profile and can show response to cueing without proving generalization or diagnosis.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What should a comprehensive fluency assessment include beyond disfluency frequency?
choices: Only words spoken per minute.
Only the listener’s severity rating.
Types and duration of disfluency, tension and secondary behaviors, variability, anticipation, avoidance, beliefs, emotional impact, participation, and personal goals.
Only family history.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: The lived impact of stuttering or cluttering can be substantial even when observable frequency is low in one context.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Before behavioral voice treatment, why is laryngeal visualization often important?
choices: Assume all dysphonia is caused by poor hydration.
Begin forceful vocal exercise without examination.
Diagnose a lesion from auditory quality alone.
Obtain appropriate medical evaluation of laryngeal structure and function so treatment is based on the underlying condition and contraindications.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which task set best supports differential motor-speech assessment?
choices: Oral motor function plus sustained phonation, alternating and sequential motion rates, repeated words, connected speech, prosody, respiration, and intelligibility.
Written spelling only.
Pure-tone screening only.
A single isolated vowel only.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Motor-speech assessment samples subsystem performance, consistency, rate, prosody, task effects, and functional communication.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Assessment of Language, Cognition, Social Communication, AAC, and Hearing
chapters.summary: Assess multimodal communication across languages, partners, environments, technologies, cognitive demands, literacy, and participation.
objectives: Describe receptive, expressive, social, cognitive, and literacy performance.
Conduct feature-matched AAC assessment without prerequisites.
Integrate hearing and multilingual access.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Language and literacy across contexts
sections.content: Assess comprehension and expression at word, sentence, narrative, expository, and conversational levels using standardized, criterion, sample, dynamic, and classroom evidence. Spoken and written language share vocabulary, syntax, phonology, narrative, and metalinguistic foundations. Evaluate all relevant languages and dialects with qualified support, appropriate opportunity and instruction data, and documented limits rather than inferring disorder from English exposure alone.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Social and cognitive communication
sections.content: Social communication changes with partner, setting, purpose, culture, sensory load, and power. Observe reciprocity, inference, perspective, discourse, nonverbal communication, repair, and self-advocacy in authentic contexts. Cognitive-communication assessment links attention, memory, executive function, awareness, fatigue, discourse, and self-monitoring with real school, work, home, and community tasks because brief impairment tests may miss participation failure.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: AAC feature matching and trials
sections.content: There are no prerequisite speech, motor, or cognitive skills for AAC access. Identify current and future communication purposes, languages, environments, partners, sensory and motor access, symbol needs, literacy, portability, durability, vocabulary, voice, funding, and preference. Trial multiple low- and high-tech options, observe learning and fatigue, include partners, document access supports, and plan reassessment as needs and abilities change.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Hearing access and communication
sections.content: Review hearing type and configuration, stability, aided access when available, technology use, communication mode, listening environment, visual supports, language exposure, and functional reports. Coordinate with audiology and education. A threshold does not describe every communication effect, and technology does not remove noise, distance, language, identity, or participation barriers. Assess speech, language, literacy, and self-advocacy as relevant.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which evidence best differentiates receptive from expressive language difficulty?
choices: Comprehension across tasks and contexts with language formulation, grammar, vocabulary, narrative, and discourse production.
Oral-motor strength only.
Swallow timing only.
Pure-tone thresholds as the sole language measure.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Receptive and expressive processes interact but require multiple tasks and functional samples to describe the pattern accurately.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What makes social-communication assessment ecologically valid?
choices: A single decontextualized vocabulary item.
Observation and sampling across authentic partners, settings, purposes, nonverbal cues, discourse demands, and cultural expectations.
The examiner’s impression without stakeholder input.
Only articulation accuracy.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Pragmatic performance varies with partner, context, power, sensory load, language background, and task expectations.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which approach best evaluates cognitive-communication after brain injury?
choices: Conclude there is no disorder from the screen.
Measure tongue strength only.
Assess functional attention, memory, executive function, discourse, self-monitoring, fatigue, environmental demands, and participation with relevant simulations.
Use an infant language scale.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Brief tests can miss complex real-world demands; assessment should connect cognitive processes with meaningful communication and daily tasks.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is feature matching in an AAC assessment?
choices: Choose the device with the largest vocabulary for everyone.
Require speech failure before considering AAC.
Select a system without trial or partner input.
Match the person’s communication needs, languages, access methods, sensory and motor abilities, environments, partners, symbols, portability, and preferences to system features.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: AAC selection is individualized and iterative; trials, communication opportunities, partner support, and changing needs shape recommendations.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which hearing information is most relevant to communication assessment?
choices: Audiologic configuration and stability, aided access when available, device use, listening environment, language exposure, communication mode, and functional reports.
Unaided threshold average alone.
The color of the hearing device.
Only the child’s articulation score.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Communication access depends on hearing status, technology, environment, linguistic experience, visual supports, and individual response.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Feeding and Swallowing Assessment
chapters.summary: Use history, clinical and instrumental evidence, interprofessional coordination, and risk triage to characterize feeding and swallowing safely.
objectives: Explain limits of clinical swallowing assessment.
Compare VFSS and FEES purposes and constraints.
Assess pediatric feeding multidimensionally.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Clinical evaluation and immediate safety
sections.content: Integrate medical, respiratory, nutritional, medication, oral-care, feeding, swallowing, and participation history with alertness, positioning, cranial-nerve and oral motor findings, secretion management, voice, cough, and trials only when appropriate. A clinical evaluation cannot visualize pharyngeal physiology or exclude silent aspiration. Stop trials and activate current emergency or medical response for respiratory compromise, acute inability to manage secretions, or other instability.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Instrumental assessment selection
sections.content: VFSS uses radiography to view dynamic oral, pharyngeal, and upper-esophageal events across selected bolus trials, with radiation, positioning, transport, and representativeness considerations. FEES uses transnasal endoscopy to examine relevant anatomy, secretions, fatigue, and repeated trials without radiation, but has a brief white-out and does not show the oral phase directly. Select the study that can answer the clinical question and change management.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Physiology, signs, and cautious inference
sections.content: Describe containment, mastication, bolus formation, oral transport, timing, airway protection, residue, clearance, efficiency, sensation, and response across relevant conditions. Cough, wet voice, or respiratory change may be warning signs but are not perfectly specific; lack of cough does not rule out aspiration. Instrumental results are samples tied to bolus, volume, posture, strategy, fatigue, health, and study conditions rather than universal truths.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Pediatric feeding and team assessment
sections.content: Pediatric feeding includes safety, nutrition, hydration, oral skill, sensory response, development, behavior, positioning, endurance, medical and gastrointestinal factors, routines, culture, caregiver–child interaction, and participation. Evaluate growth and nutrition with appropriate professionals and respect family foods, access, and goals. Avoid reducing a complex feeding problem to willful refusal or a count of accepted foods without functional and medical context.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is the purpose of a clinical swallowing evaluation?
choices: Integrate history, alertness, respiratory and oral status, cranial-nerve and motor findings, trials when appropriate, signs, function, risk, and need for referral.
Directly visualize every pharyngeal event.
Rule out silent aspiration with certainty.
Replace medical and nutritional assessment.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Clinical evaluation identifies patterns and risk but cannot directly visualize swallowing physiology or exclude silent aspiration.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which instrumental study uses dynamic radiography to examine bolus flow through oral, pharyngeal, and upper-esophageal swallowing events?
choices: A pure-tone audiogram.
A videofluoroscopic swallowing study (VFSS), when clinically indicated and performed with appropriate team and radiation considerations.
A laryngeal perceptual rating only.
A standardized vocabulary test.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: VFSS provides dynamic radiographic information about bolus flow and physiology and can test selected strategies under study conditions.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which statement accurately identifies a major advantage of FEES?
choices: VFSS continuously displays pharyngeal swallowing without a white-out period or ionizing radiation.
Pure-tone audiometry directly visualizes laryngeal anatomy and bolus flow.
FEES can be performed portably and can assess relevant anatomy, secretions, fatigue, and repeated trials without ionizing radiation when clinically appropriate.
FEES directly images the oral preparatory phase and upper esophagus throughout every swallow.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: FEES offers portable endoscopic assessment of relevant anatomy and swallowing before and after the brief white-out period.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why can aspiration be missed during a bedside examination?
choices: Absence of cough proves airway protection.
Aspiration always produces immediate fever.
Voice quality alone locates the impairment.
Aspiration may be silent, and clinical signs have limited sensitivity for defining pharyngeal physiology.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Silent aspiration and nonspecific signs require risk-based clinical reasoning and instrumental evaluation when results would change management.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Prolonged mastication, poor bolus formation, and oral residue most directly implicate which swallowing phases?
choices: The oral preparatory and oral transit phases.
The esophageal phase only.
Auditory processing.
Receptive language.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Oral-phase function includes containment, mastication, bolus preparation, and lingual transport, though impairments can span phases.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Treatment Planning, Goal Design, Outcomes, and Counseling
chapters.domain: Planning, Implementation, and Evaluation of Treatment
chapters.summary: Translate assessment into shared priorities, measurable goals, justified dosage, generalization, progress decisions, prognosis, and continuity.
objectives: Write measurable functional goals.
Individualize treatment mechanism and dosage.
Use outcome data to revise or conclude care.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Priorities, goals, and shared decisions
sections.content: Prioritize safety and urgent needs, desired participation, functional impact, developmental or recovery leverage, and individual and family goals. A measurable goal names the behavior, context, support, performance criterion, measurement method, and review period while remaining meaningful. Explain evidence, alternatives, burden, risk, and uncertainty accessibly so the person can help choose targets and methods instead of being asked only to comply.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Mechanism, dosage, and prognosis
sections.content: Match treatment to an assessed mechanism rather than diagnosis name alone. Select frequency, session length, intensity, distribution, and duration using severity, learning response, health, fatigue, evidence, access, burden, environment, and preferences. Prognosis is conditional: communicate favorable and limiting factors, a reasonable range, uncertainty, meaningful endpoints, and how response data will update the estimate without guarantees.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Practice, feedback, and generalization
sections.content: Acquisition performance is not the same as learning. Adjust practice schedule, task complexity, cueing, knowledge of results or performance, and response frequency as the learner changes. Program generalization by varying materials, partners, settings, linguistic load, and natural consequences; train self-monitoring and partners; and measure untrained tasks and real participation. Repeating one successful drill does not demonstrate transfer.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Progress decisions, discharge, and follow-up
sections.content: Graph a sensitive outcome against the goal and pair it with fidelity, dosage, participation, burden, and adverse-effect data. If progress is weak, verify measurement and implementation, reassess the hypothesis and access, consult the person and team, and modify or refer. Discharge uses current criteria, benefit and burden, function, preferences, alternatives, education, referrals, and a documented follow-up or reentry plan rather than a fixed visit count.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which treatment goal is most measurable?
choices: Specify the functional behavior, context, level of support, performance criterion, measurement method, and review period.
Improve communication soon.
Demonstrate normal language at all times.
Participate better without defining evidence.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Measurable goals connect a meaningful functional outcome with observable conditions and data while remaining revisable through shared decision making.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why is a baseline necessary before treatment?
choices: A guarantee of eventual success.
A representative measure of current performance under defined conditions against which progress can be compared.
A billing code only.
The clinician’s memory of typical performance.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Baseline data clarify starting level, variability, task conditions, goal calibration, and later interpretation of change.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should treatment frequency, intensity, and duration be selected?
choices: Use one fixed schedule for every diagnosis.
Select the maximum possible dosage without monitoring.
Individualize dosage using goals, severity, learning response, health, fatigue, setting, evidence, access, burden, preferences, and ongoing outcome data.
Base dosage only on clinician convenience.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Dosage is a clinical variable that should be justified and adjusted through evidence, feasibility, response, and person-centered priorities.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which factor should receive highest priority when selecting initial treatment targets?
choices: The first item on the test protocol.
The easiest behavior for the clinician to score.
A target unrelated to daily communication.
Safety and urgent needs, functional participation, individual and family goals, developmental or recovery leverage, stimulability, and likelihood of meaningful generalization.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Target selection integrates urgency, meaningfulness, readiness, evidence, environment, and the person’s desired outcomes.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is shared decision making in treatment planning?
choices: Explain evidence and uncertainty accessibly, elicit values and preferences, compare options, and agree on a plan and review point.
Choose privately and ask for compliance.
Provide only the option preferred by the clinician.
Avoid discussing uncertainty.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Shared decision making combines professional evidence with informed individual and care-partner priorities and preserves autonomy.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Treatment of Speech Sound, Fluency, Voice, Resonance, and Motor Speech
chapters.domain: Planning, Implementation, and Evaluation of Treatment
chapters.summary: Select mechanism-matched speech, fluency, voice, resonance, and motor-speech interventions and measure retention, transfer, and participation.
objectives: Match speech-sound approaches to error patterns.
Center fluency treatment on speaker priorities.
Coordinate voice, resonance, and motor-speech care safely.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Speech sound contrasts and motor learning
sections.content: Select an approach from the pattern. Minimal pairs can restore a missing contrast; broader pattern approaches may suit multiple processes; motor-based work uses accurate models, placement and movement cues, many responses, and feedback. Choose targets using intelligibility, system leverage, stimulability, developmental and linguistic relevance, and person priorities. Measure untrained words and connected speech rather than only practiced productions.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Fluency, agency, and participation
sections.content: Collaboratively address the speaker’s desired outcomes, which may include easier speech, reduced struggle, knowledge, self-advocacy, disclosure choices, acceptance, communication confidence, participation, or selected fluency strategies. Avoid promising cure or treating listener comfort as the only outcome. Include real speaking situations, variability, anticipation and avoidance, family or teacher support, and measures meaningful to the speaker.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Voice and resonance management
sections.content: After appropriate laryngeal evaluation, voice treatment can coordinate respiration, phonation, and resonance, reduce harmful effort, and build efficient function for actual vocal demands. Monitor symptoms, load, adherence, response, and need for medical follow-up. Articulation treatment cannot close a structural velopharyngeal gap; coordinate structural management while treating learned compensatory placements separately and measuring resonance and intelligibility.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Dysarthria, apraxia, and communication support
sections.content: Dysarthria management may address respiration, phonation, resonance, articulation, prosody, rate, loudness, clear speech, partner strategies, environment, and AAC based on subsystem findings and disease course. Apraxia treatment emphasizes repeated accurate speech movements, integral stimulation or other evidence-aligned cueing, prosody, and systematic fading. Provide communication supports early rather than withholding AAC until speech normalizes.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: When is a minimal-pairs approach especially appropriate?
choices: Use meaningful word pairs that differ by the target contrast when the child can benefit from contrastive phonological learning.
Use swallowing maneuvers unrelated to speech.
Practice only nonspeech oral movements.
Teach vocabulary definitions without producing the contrast.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Minimal pairs can establish the communicative consequence of a contrast, but approach selection depends on error pattern, severity, stimulability, and system needs.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which practice best promotes motor learning for an articulatory placement target?
choices: No feedback during acquisition.
Accurate models and cues, many meaningful response opportunities, knowledge of performance initially, and systematic adjustment of practice and feedback as learning develops.
Only passive listening.
Randomly changing the target after each error.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Motor learning distinguishes temporary performance from retention and transfer and uses feedback and practice schedules strategically.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is a person-centered goal for stuttering treatment?
choices: Promise complete fluency as the only acceptable outcome.
Require concealment of stuttering.
Address communication participation, agency, knowledge, avoidance and tension as desired, effective disclosure or self-advocacy, and speech-management options chosen by the speaker.
Measure success only by listener ratings.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Fluency care should reflect the speaker’s priorities and can target impact, participation, ease, self-efficacy, and acceptance as well as speech behaviors.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: After appropriate laryngeal evaluation, what is the purpose of physiologic voice therapy?
choices: Maximize loudness regardless of tissue response.
Use prolonged whispering as a universal treatment.
Treat every diagnosis with the same exercise.
Coordinate respiration, phonation, and resonance to reduce harmful effort and improve efficient voice for functional demands.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Voice treatment is diagnosis- and person-specific and monitors symptoms, workload, technique, adherence, and transfer.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which intervention is often appropriate for dysarthria with reduced intelligibility?
choices: Target relevant speech subsystems and compensatory strategies such as rate, loudness, articulation, phrasing, partner supports, and AAC based on assessment.
Treat language comprehension only.
Use one strategy regardless of dysarthria type.
Delay communication support until speech is normal.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Dysarthria management can combine restorative and compensatory approaches across subsystems and participation needs.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Treatment of Language, Cognition, Social Communication, AAC, and Hearing
chapters.domain: Planning, Implementation, and Evaluation of Treatment
chapters.summary: Build functional language, literacy, cognitive-communication, social, AAC, hearing, and partner supports across authentic activities.
objectives: Connect language treatment to curriculum and participation.
Teach cognitive and social strategies in real contexts.
Support AAC and hearing access without unsupported prerequisites.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Language, literacy, and curriculum participation
sections.content: Select vocabulary, syntax, narrative, expository, phonological, and metalinguistic targets that affect meaningful communication and learning. Use explicit instruction, models, guided practice, feedback, multiple examples, retrieval, and application in speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Coordinate with educators and families, respect the student’s languages and dialects, and measure classroom or daily participation rather than isolated test-like responses alone.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Aphasia and cognitive-communication rehabilitation
sections.content: Aphasia care can combine impairment-focused language work with multimodal strategies, environmental supports, partner training, identity and counseling support, and personally meaningful participation. Cognitive-communication treatment uses external aids, routines, metacognitive strategies, structured practice, environmental modification, and relevant task simulations. Match support to awareness, fatigue, memory, executive demands, and the person’s goals and contexts.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Social communication and partner intervention
sections.content: Develop goals with the person, not solely for conformity to listener expectations. Practice relevant interaction, inference, repair, perspective, boundaries, self-advocacy, and communication choices with authentic partners and environments. Make expectations explicit while respecting neurodiversity, culture, identity, and harmless differences. Partner training can change opportunities, pacing, cues, confirmation, and environmental support instead of placing all responsibility on the communicator.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: AAC and aural rehabilitation
sections.content: Provide AAC without prerequisite tests or prolonged waiting. Use aided language modeling, robust vocabulary, reliable access, multiple communication functions, partner responsiveness, and frequent opportunities across environments; retain speech and other modalities as desired. Aural rehabilitation integrates hearing technology with auditory and visual access, environmental changes, communication strategies, language or speech needs, partner training, and self-advocacy through SLP–audiology collaboration.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which school-language intervention is most likely to generalize to academic participation?
choices: Teach relevant vocabulary, syntax, narrative or expository structures, and metalinguistic strategies using curriculum-connected tasks with educator and family collaboration.
Practice unrelated word lists only.
Remove classroom communication demands permanently.
Target articulation without evidence of a speech-sound need.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Language intervention should connect underlying skills and strategies with meaningful curriculum and communication contexts.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is a life-participation approach to aphasia?
choices: Decontextualized naming as the only outcome.
Meaningful communication activities, environmental supports, partner training, multimodal strategies, impairment work as relevant, and personally chosen participation outcomes.
Speech-only communication despite effective alternatives.
Goals selected without the person’s input.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Aphasia treatment can combine restorative and compensatory methods while centering autonomy, identity, relationships, access, and participation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which intervention best supports prospective memory after brain injury?
choices: Rely on repeated verbal reminders forever.
Use articulation drills only.
Use external aids, routines, environmental cues, error-aware strategy training, and practice in real tasks while measuring independence and generalization.
Avoid compensatory technology.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Cognitive-communication intervention matches strategy, task, environment, awareness, and support and measures functional performance.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is a strong social-communication intervention principle?
choices: Teach one rigid script for every context.
Require masking of harmless differences as the sole goal.
Exclude the student’s priorities.
Collaboratively practice relevant interaction strategies with natural partners and settings, explicit reflection, multiple perspectives, self-advocacy, and authentic outcome measures.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Social communication goals should be functional, culturally responsive, autonomy-supportive, and relevant to the person’s desired participation.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Which statement about AAC candidacy is correct?
choices: There are no prerequisite cognitive or speech skills for access to AAC; assessment should identify supports and a communication system now.
AAC should wait until speech therapy fails for years.
AAC prevents speech development.
Only individuals who can spell may use AAC.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: AAC can support communication, language, participation, and speech development; access should not be withheld by unsupported prerequisites.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

chapters.title: Feeding and Swallowing Treatment
chapters.domain: Planning, Implementation, and Evaluation of Treatment
chapters.summary: Select individualized compensatory, rehabilitative, nutritional, environmental, and caregiver supports from assessed physiology and shared goals.
objectives: Distinguish compensation from rehabilitation.
Evaluate texture, exercise, and oral-care decisions multidimensionally.
Support responsive pediatric feeding and informed team decisions.
chapters.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Compensation, rehabilitation, and mechanism
sections.content: Compensatory strategies change performance during use through posture, bolus, pacing, cueing, or environmental adjustment; rehabilitation aims for durable physiologic change through appropriately loaded practice or exercise. Select either from assessed impairment, contraindications, health, person ability, evidence, and goals. Verify benefit for the individual and bolus rather than generalizing a strategy from another patient or a single diagnosis.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Texture, hydration, oral care, and risk
sections.content: Modified food or liquid may improve one aspect of safety while increasing residue, dehydration, medication, access, adherence, or quality-of-life burden. Discuss alternatives and preferences, provide clear preparation guidance, and reassess. Effective oral hygiene and dental care can reduce oral bacterial load as one part of pulmonary-risk management, but no single intervention prevents all aspiration-related complications.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Exercise dosage and outcome monitoring
sections.content: Choose an exercise only when its physiologic target matches documented impairment and the person can perform it safely. Specify load, repetitions, frequency, rest, progression, adherence, and stop criteria with medical coordination when needed. Measure more than a clinic maneuver: include physiology when indicated, airway and medical events, nutrition, hydration, efficiency, intake, burden, adherence, participation, quality of life, and person-selected outcomes.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

sections.heading: Responsive pediatric feeding and alternate nutrition
sections.content: Pediatric intervention integrates safety and nutrition with developmentally appropriate oral skill, positioning, sensory access, predictable routines, caregiver coaching, autonomy, and reduced pressure. Respect culture and family foods and avoid coercion. Alternate nutrition may support hydration and nutrition but does not automatically eliminate aspiration of secretions or reflux; placement and oral-access decisions require informed interprofessional discussion of goals, risks, prognosis, and quality of life.
sections.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What distinguishes a compensatory swallowing strategy from a rehabilitative exercise?
choices: It is compensatory; it aims to improve safety or efficiency during use, whereas rehabilitation seeks durable change in function.
It is automatically rehabilitative because it involves movement.
It cures the underlying disease.
It is a language intervention.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Compensatory and rehabilitative approaches have different mechanisms and must be selected from assessed physiology, goals, and evidence.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should a postural swallowing strategy be prescribed?
choices: Recommend the same posture for every patient because its effects are identical.
Use a strategy only when assessment shows benefit for that individual and bolus, teach it accurately, and monitor safety, efficiency, adherence, and unintended effects.
Use the posture without explaining its purpose or checking response.
Avoid every posture because no postural change can affect swallowing.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Postures alter anatomy and bolus flow differently across impairments, so individual verification and monitoring are essential.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: What is required when recommending modified food or liquid consistency?
choices: Assume thicker is always safer.
Ignore dehydration and burden.
Physiology, hydration and nutrition, medication delivery, residue, preference, quality of life, access, adherence, alternatives, informed choice, and ongoing reassessment.
Use the recommendation without shared decision making.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Texture modification can have benefits and harms; individualized evidence, education, monitoring, and interprofessional review are required.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: Why is oral care relevant to dysphagia management?
choices: Eliminate all oral intake without assessment.
Use oral care only when pain occurs.
Assume clean teeth prevent all aspiration.
Consistent effective oral hygiene and dental care as part of a broader individualized risk-reduction plan.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Oral bacterial load is one contributor to aspiration-related pulmonary complications, alongside health, mobility, dependence, and swallowing factors.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

knowledgeChecks.prompt: How should a swallowing exercise be selected?
choices: Match the exercise’s physiologic target and dosage to documented impairment, contraindications, patient ability and goals, evidence, and measured response.
Use every exercise for every dysphagia pattern.
Choose the most difficult exercise regardless of impairment.
Continue despite pain or medical instability.
knowledgeChecks.rationale: Exercise should have a plausible and assessed mechanism, safe dosage, appropriate load, adherence support, and outcome monitoring.
knowledgeChecks.reviewNote: Independently authored and source reviewed for study use; independent licensed-SLP validation remains pending.

memoryAids.title: Speech Subsystems: Power, Source, Filter, Valve
memoryAids.content: Respiration supplies power, vocal folds provide the source, the vocal tract filters sound, and the velopharyngeal valve directs oral and nasal energy.
